mappings.rationaleAI: Renaissance mathematics covers recovery of Greek texts (A.1.b.i), introduction of modern notation (A.2.b), perspective geometry relating to projective geometry (A.2.d.iii), and the solution of cubic and quartic equations by Cardano, Tartaglia, and Ferrari (A.2.e.iii, A.2.e.iv).
Euler's number e, developed by Bernoulli and Euler, is central to 18th-century advances in analysis (A.1.b.ii), connecting to logarithms, infinite series, and the exponential function.

Imaginary numbers trace from Cardano and Bombelli's work on cubic equations (A.2.e.iii) through Euler's notation and Gauss's complex plane, contributing to 19th-century developments in complex variables (A.1.b.iii).

Cantor's set theory, transfinite numbers, and the continuum hypothesis are foundational for the concept of cardinal number and set theory (B.2.b), while the foundational crisis and debates between formalism, intuitionism, and logicism address the crisis after 1900 (B.3).

mappings.episodeTitle: Maths in the Early Islamic World
mappings.rationaleAI: Islamic mathematics covers al-Khwarizmi's algebra and algorithms (A.2.e.ii), Arabic numerals and the decimal system (A.2.a.ii), and the transmission of mathematical knowledge from the Islamic world to medieval Europe (A.1.a.iii).

Zero's conceptual history from Babylonian placeholder through Indian mathematics and Arabic transmission traces the development of positional numeral systems (A.2.a.ii) and contributions from India and the Islamic world to algebra (A.2.e.ii).

Euclid's Elements systematized Greek geometry (A.1.a.ii, A.2.d.i), established the axiomatic-deductive method foundational for Euclidean geometry (B.1.a), and challenges to the parallel postulate led to non-Euclidean geometry (B.1.b).

Negative numbers trace their earlier acceptance in Chinese and Indian mathematics (A.2.e.ii) and the gradual development of algebraic understanding in medieval and modern European mathematics (A.2.e.iii).

Indian mathematics covers the decimal place-value system and zero (A.2.a.ii), Aryabhata's trigonometry and algebra (A.2.e.ii), the Kerala school's anticipation of calculus, and transmission to the Islamic world (A.1.a.iii).

Probability theory from Pascal and Fermat through Bernoulli, Laplace, and Kolmogorov traces the development of mathematical probability (A.3.a), including alternative views of probability (A.3.a.ii) and Bernoulli's theorem (A.3.a.iii).

The history of formal logic from Boole through Frege and Russell connects to 19th-20th century foundations of mathematics (A.1.b.iii), and Goedel's incompleteness theorems bear directly on the foundational crisis (B.3, B.3.c).

Gauss's contributions span number theory (A.2.e.iv), non-Euclidean geometry (A.2.d.iv), the method of least squares in statistics (A.3.b), and 18th-19th century advances in analysis (A.1.b.ii, A.1.b.iii).
Fermat's Last Theorem and Wiles's proof using Galois representations and modularity theorem represent landmark developments in number theory and algebraic geometry (A.2.e.iv, A.1.b.iii).

The development of calculus by Newton and Leibniz in the late 17th century, including the priority dispute and the philosophical implications of infinitesimals, is a defining event in the history of mathematics (A.1.b.i).
Pi's history from Archimedes' polygon method through Madhava's infinite series to modern computation traces developments in Greek geometry (A.1.a.ii, A.2.d.i) and analysis across centuries.

The broad discussion of mathematics as a discipline, the axiomatic method, and debates about realism, formalism, and foundations connects to the overall history of mathematics (A.1) and foundations (B, B.3).
Archimedes' calculation of pi, the method of exhaustion anticipating integral calculus, and his mathematical achievements are central to Greek and Hellenistic mathematics (A.1.a.ii, A.2.d.i).
Emmy Noether's foundational contributions to abstract algebra including ring theory and ideal theory represent major 20th-century developments in algebra (A.1.b.iii).

Pythagoras's discovery of mathematical ratios and the Pythagorean theorem are foundational for Greek mathematics (A.1.a.ii), and the belief that reality is fundamentally mathematical connects to philosophical aspects of geometry (A.2.d.i).
Pythagoras's discovery of mathematical ratios in musical intervals represents an early application of mathematics in Greek civilization (A.1.a.ii) connecting number theory to natural phenomena.
Zeno's paradoxes engaged with infinity, continuity, and the foundations of calculus, representing important Greek mathematical thinking (A.1.a.ii) that influenced subsequent mathematical development.

The mathematical structures underlying music, from Pythagoras's harmonic ratios to Fourier analysis and group theory in composition, trace mathematical developments from Greek mathematics (A.1.a.ii) through modern analysis.
